author: @isettech
text: Thanks for the correction. I watched the first video and wondered where are the 6 poles?  Instead of joining the two coils end to end or in parallel, the other coil can be continued, but reverse the direction to cross the original coil to make the poles. Yes there is a way to do this without cutting the wire for only two ends. Due to the segments and the crossing of the wire, it will make poles in the "First winding, and the reverse direction, fills the gaps and produces poles that align in both coils in one wire.  It is a little mental gymnastics to picture it, but when you join the ends to series the two windings, you will see it. Wind the first progressing CCW as viewed from the camera, then without stopping when you reach 1/2 way, wind back to fill the spaces, but this time in a clockwise direction, this will produce a cross pattern with your required poles in the winding. Hope this helps.

Does the charge on the circuit reverse as the field on the wire reverses? I can understand how they would cancel out but not flow. A guy made a welder out of a compressor motor for a refrigerator and he wrapped a wire around a toilet paper roller 8 times. That wire would have reversing charges too.

author: @alt3241
text:
In the past I used a " growler " ( an AC fed electromagnet )
1. a simple setup having a bicolor LED dropping resistance at a test lead near the probe end and a MOSFET or relay ( the coil of which can be your exciter coil ) powered ( at low frequencies) through a diode  providing connection on single exciter polarity 
or 
2. a fancy setup having given an audio specrum frequency with a reference coil of the similar ( not exactly the same so that you hear connection/ action ) reactance to connect nearly cancel to a speaker output ( headphones ( unless wireless) could be lethal proximity at high voltages ) with a common connection and attenuator and or limiter you can hear all the phase relationships related to the other end of the coils as you touch the test leads .
For motor construction you can use a biphase growler as an exciter or use different tones on the two electromagnets but this is much more complicated but can use stereo speakers and or oscilloscope x and y inputs .

replies.author: @alt3241
replies.text: Growlers used typically in motor , tranformer and alternator rebuilding .

replies.author: @alt3241
replies.text: avoid using growlers on current or high voltage transformers and autodyne or motor/generators without proper output termination to prevent insulation breakdown of high voltage coils !

author: @lisakingscott7729
text: Hi Rob, I'm not sure your reasoning for using Litz wire is correct, particularly at 10s of Hz and the low currents involved here. More thin wires in parallel with a given overall cross section will pass through the same number of magnetic field lines as a solid wire with the same cross sectional area and produce / carry the same current.

author: @janbeute4902
text: The Rodin Coil defines the conservation of momentum and energy. It has a perfectly stable voltage output. When a Rodin Coil transformer receives 50 watts input it creates a 250 watts output! It can be stacked and looped for larger power needs and will easily produce 5, 10 or 15 kilowatts output of electrical power to run a business or a home or an entire industrial complex. Completely off-grid with no solar, wind turbine, or batteries required. The Rodin Coil Over Unity Energy Amplifier turns milliamps into kilowatts. It is cheap to manufacture and costs only pennies when mass produced. It uses entirely off-the-shelf components, requires no special tooling and uses no exotic or proprietary materials.

author: @wubwub89
text:
Considering the context, I think it's a reasonable omission. Yes, it's absolutely relevant in practice and design, but I would doubt most who take an interest in 'less conventional' (for lack of creative language) winding structure and field/stator geometry would not be aware of phase conventions and control/drive methodology, or flip-flop it for an alternator/generator with a rectifier array, as others have said. Getting into the electrodynamics of winding wire and it's physical characteristics, in my opinion, is  reasonably basic enough to leave out on a slightly higher level demonstration of concept. This one may be a tiny bit tricky for newbies on phase orientation for a delta wind if they jump into it without drawing it out first... especially if their only experience is with brushed DC, or using capacitors on single phase AC drives. 

I know this is mostly implied by some of the comments, in the negative space of things, but I think this design (assuming low Q/freq and losses not of concern, as an academic exercise) would be interesting using copper foil (whatever hits right at the knee of surface area : ideal inductance for X application) instead of stranded wire. I've had a few interesting results with foil windings, mostly just messing around, albeit that requires some extra math to do it 'right'. 

There's already a lot of great comments here with additional information, I just wanted to share my thoughts. And be the weirdo who suggests testing foil/bar windings just to make all of the engineers scrunch their faces up.

author: @Enjoymentboy
text: Watching this video I THINK you managed to solve a problem I've had (mentioned in the comments of your last video) on how to wire up using some old SCSI HDD magnets...so thank you for that.  But I also recently found a couple dozen 100' rolls of 1/2" copper foil tape (used for magnetic shielding I think) and I wonder just how well this stuff could be used instead of wire.  It would make a fairly narrow and reasonably thin coil that would have many turns and a lot of copper mass.  With one side being adhesive backed I THINK it might serve as a decent insulation as well, but that is purely a guess on my part.  Thoughts?

replies.author: @Twistedmetal-qe8kx
replies.text: Unfortunately the tape is like one really wide wire, so it will only  count as one winding each layer.  It would carry a lot of current though.

replies.author: @jbarker2160
replies.text: You need to check.  Some copper ribbon has conductive adhesive, especially if it's intended for shielding.

replies.author: @seditt5146
replies.text: don't expect the adhesive to be useful.  instead at least place saran wrap or something in between each layer.  As stated this is a single thick wire but can be useful in electromagnet when running a ton of current through it.  i found its best use as dyi capacitor creation for various HV usages. Two layers of foil with appropriate insulator like that mentioned above then coiled up and placed inside of pvc tube filled with glycerin or some oil and the end result is a relatively great capacitor.  Be careful as they can be rather powerful and knock your socks off if not careful.

author: @ChristieNel
text: Wouldn't thicker wire be the same as litz wire?  They'd handle the same current?  Is the field strength different?

replies.author: @Kangsteri
replies.text: No. Voltage is surface tension. Litz wire has much more surface area. One wire works well on low frequency DC (when current is below the surface). Litz wire is better for high frequency AC (when current moves above the surface).

replies.author: @ChristieNel
replies.text: @Kangsteri I know litz will give more surface area and be better at high frequency, but it was said that the goal was not high frequency. So why?

replies.author: @Kangsteri
replies.text: @ChristieNel As he stated, this way you don't need to wind them separately. I believe it will work better as pickup coil cause it's always AC in the coil, before rectification.

replies.author: @ChristieNel
replies.text: @Kangsteri So it works better because it's AC, regardless of frequency? Because otherwise you could just use one thicker wire and not need to wire them separately either.

replies.author: @Kangsteri
replies.text: @ChristieNel Yes it will most likely work better on all AC. It's often used on low voltage pulse motors and crystal radios too. I'm interested to see how he's going to apply these, or are they just for experiments...

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Ideally I'd say use one solid strand of insulated wire if it were not relatively difficult to bend and work with. It would be better because its cross section fills more space with with very low resistance current carrying  copper in parallel rather than high resistance insulation inside a bundle of litz wire with many smaller copper paths in running in parallel. 

An insulated stranded wire is effectivly exactly the same as a litz wire at low freqency, except that litz wire has insulation on every strand consuming space that could be filled with copper.  where as insulated stranded wire fills that space more with  current carrying copper than lits wire of the same outer diameter while having insulation only on the outside. But, it might be dificult to get insulated stranded wire with the outside insulation thin enough to maximize the copper inside for the given diameter. In that case litz wire is fine. But otherwise, at these low frequencies, stranded wire and litz wire with the same copper cross section will have the same resistance and virtually the same current carrying capacity. 

Thank you for the mention Robert.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @BrentLeVasseur
replies.text: I think the problem with that is the thickness of the outer insulation of the multi-strand wire, which in most cases is a lot thicker than a bundle of lightly coated litz wire strands. The reason being is when you have a thicker dialectic outer coating in a coil you are increasing capacitance - essentially making a coil capacitor, which means more energy is being stored as a voltage potential instead of being used to drive torque or spin in the motor. It’s basically the same thing as having an air gap between the stator and rotor. It adds unwanted capacitance while killing the thing you want, which is torque.
